title: Reflection on Using AI Tools for Portfolio Development
text:
Building this portfolio website with Quarto was my first real experience using AI tools for a complete project, and it taught me a lot about both the benefits and limitations of these technologies.


When I first started thinking about how to structure a portfolio website, I honestly wasn’t sure where to begin. I used ChatGPT to help brainstorm what pages I should include and what kind of content would make sense for each section. The AI suggested a five-page layout (Home, Resume, Projects, Skills, Contact) which seemed pretty standard for portfolios, so I went with that.
The AI was helpful in giving me a starting framework. It suggested what should go on each page - like putting highlights on the home page and organizing my resume in a traditional format. This saved me time because I didn’t have to research portfolio best practices from scratch.



Once I had the structure, I asked the AI to help draft content for each page. This is where things got interesting. The AI created initial drafts that were… okay, but very generic. For example, when describing my work at Appsyco, it used really formal language and made everything sound more impressive than it actually was.
I had to go through and rewrite a lot of it to sound like me and to be accurate about what I actually did. I added specific details about the forecasting projects I worked on and the dividend analysis tasks. The AI couldn’t know these details - only I could provide them.
The tone was another issue. The AI writes in this very polished, professional way that doesn’t really sound like how I would naturally explain my experience. I spent time making it more conversational while still keeping it professional enough for a portfolio.



The biggest advantage was speed. The AI generated a complete website structure and initial content in maybe 15-20 minutes. If I had started from scratch, it probably would have taken me several hours just to figure out the layout and write first drafts.
The AI was also really good at technical stuff like creating the CSS styling and making sure all the Markdown formatting was correct. I don’t have much web development experience, so having the AI handle the styling saved me from having to learn CSS in depth.



The main limitation was that AI can’t actually know anything about my personal experiences. Every specific detail about my education, my projects, and my work had to come from me. The AI could create a template, but I had to fill in the real substance.
Another challenge was that the AI couldn’t help me decide what to emphasize or how to position myself for the kind of jobs I want. Those strategic decisions required me to think about my career goals and what employers in analytics are looking for.



This project showed me that AI tools are really useful for getting started and handling technical tasks, but they can’t replace the human element. The final portfolio needed my personal touch, my actual experiences, and my judgment about what matters.
I think the best approach is using AI as a starting point and then putting in the work to make it authentic and personal. The AI helped me build something faster than I could have alone, but the value comes from the parts that are genuinely mine.
(Word count: 498)

title: Reflection: Using AI Tools to Build a Portfolio Website
text: Creating this portfolio website using Quarto and Markdown was an enlightening experience that demonstrated both the capabilities and limitations of Large Language Model (LLM) tools in web development and content creation. This reflection explores how AI assistance shaped the development process, the editorial decisions made, and the lessons learned about human-AI collaboration.

title: Reflection: Using AI Tools to Build a Portfolio Website
text:
The initial challenge was conceptualizing a professional portfolio structure that would effectively showcase my skills, experience, and projects. I used an LLM tool to brainstorm the optimal page organization for an MBA student’s portfolio. The AI suggested a five-page structure: Home/About, Resume, Projects, Skills, and Contact. This recommendation was based on best practices for professional portfolios and aligned perfectly with my goal of presenting a comprehensive professional profile.
The LLM also helped identify what content should appear on each page. For instance, it suggested that the home page should include key highlights and a professional introduction, while the resume page should follow a traditional format with education, experience, and career goals. This structural guidance provided a solid foundation that I could then customize with my specific information.

title: Reflection: Using AI Tools to Build a Portfolio Website
text:
The AI tool generated initial drafts for all pages, including descriptions of my BYD market entry project and my work experience at Appsyco. However, these drafts required significant editing and verification. I carefully reviewed each section to ensure accuracy, adding specific details about my actual responsibilities and achievements that only I could provide.
For example, the AI-generated content about my data analyst role was generic. I enhanced it by adding specific contributions like “supported sales forecasting analysis using historical data” and “enhanced data processing workflows to improve team efficiency.” These details made the content more authentic and credible.
I also edited the tone and style throughout the website. The AI sometimes used overly formal language or repetitive phrasing. I refined the content to sound more natural and conversational while maintaining professionalism. The skills page, in particular, required reorganization to better categorize my technical, analytical, and business competencies.

title: Reflection: Using AI Tools to Build a Portfolio Website
text:
The primary benefit was efficiency. The AI tool generated a complete website structure in minutes, which would have taken hours to create from scratch. It also provided creative suggestions I might not have considered, such as including a professional development section on the resume page and organizing skills into distinct categories.
The AI excelled at creating consistent formatting across all pages and ensuring proper Markdown syntax. It also generated a professional CSS stylesheet that provided responsive design and modern styling without requiring deep CSS knowledge.

title: Reflection: Using AI Tools to Build a Portfolio Website
text:
Despite its strengths, the AI tool had notable limitations. It could not verify the accuracy of personal information or provide genuine insights about my experiences. All specific details about my education, work history, and projects required manual input and verification.
The AI also struggled with nuance and context. It sometimes generated content that was technically correct but didn’t capture my personal voice or the unique aspects of my professional journey. This required careful editing to ensure the portfolio authentically represented me.
Additionally, the AI couldn’t make subjective decisions about what to emphasize or how to position my experience for specific career goals. These strategic choices required human judgment based on my understanding of the job market and my career aspirations.

title: Reflection: Using AI Tools to Build a Portfolio Website
text:
Using LLM tools to build this portfolio website was a valuable exercise in human-AI collaboration. The AI provided an excellent starting point, handling structural decisions and generating initial content efficiently. However, the final product required substantial human oversight, editing, and personalization. The experience reinforced that AI tools are powerful assistants but not replacements for human creativity, judgment, and authentic personal expression. The most effective approach combines AI efficiency with human expertise and personal insight.
Word Count: 487 words

title: Skills
text:
Technical Skills
Tools I use regularly:

Python for data analysis and automation
Excel for financial modeling and data analysis (pivot tables, advanced formulas)
Tableau for creating dashboards and visualizations
Quarto for documentation

I’m also comfortable with statistical analysis, data cleaning, database queries, and basic version control.


Analytical Abilities
I enjoy working with data to find insights that actually matter for business decisions. Some areas where I’ve developed skills:

Business analytics and turning data into actionable insights
Marketing analytics including customer segmentation and campaign analysis
Forecasting and time series analysis
Market research and competitive intelligence
Data visualization to tell stories with data

I’ve also learned various analytical frameworks through my MBA like SWOT analysis, Porter’s Five Forces, financial ratio analysis, and regression analysis.


Business Skills
Beyond the technical stuff, I think what makes analysis valuable is being able to connect it to business strategy. I try to focus on:

Understanding the actual business problem before diving into data
Communicating findings in a way that non-technical stakeholders can understand
Thinking critically about what the data is really telling us
Collaborating with different teams to get the full picture



Areas of Interest
Through my coursework and work experience, I’ve been exposed to:

Strategic management and marketing strategy
Financial analysis
The electric vehicle market (through my BYD project)
Technology sector and e-commerce analytics

I’m always trying to learn more and stay current with what’s happening in business analytics and data science.

title: Projects
text:
For my MBA program, I worked on analyzing how BYD could enter the Canadian electric vehicle market. This was a really interesting project because it involved looking at everything from competitive dynamics to regulatory challenges.
What I focused on:
The main goal was to figure out if and how BYD could successfully compete in Canada’s EV market. I looked at Tesla’s dominance, analyzed foreign investment policies, evaluated trade tariffs, and even considered launch opportunities like the Vancouver International Auto Expo.
I also spent time understanding Canadian consumer preferences and what infrastructure would be needed for BYD to actually succeed here.
What I learned:
This project taught me a lot about how market research and data analysis come together in strategic planning. The biggest takeaway was understanding that successful market entry isn’t just about having a good product - it’s about timing, positioning, and navigating regulatory environments.



At Appsyco, I worked on two main types of projects: sales forecasting and dividend analysis for shareholders.
Sales Forecasting:
I helped analyze historical sales data to predict future trends. This involved using Excel to build models, running time series analysis, and creating visualizations that made the data easier to understand for stakeholders. It was interesting to see how accurate predictions could help with resource planning.
Dividend Analysis:
The dividend work was about helping the company figure out sustainable dividend policies. I looked at financial ratios, analyzed cash flow patterns, and helped put together reports for quarterly reviews.
What made this valuable:
Working on these projects showed me how data analysis directly impacts business decisions. The forecasts we created actually influenced how the company allocated resources, and the dividend analysis helped inform shareholder communications.



During my MBA, I’ve also worked on case studies covering marketing strategy, consumer behavior, and business process optimization. Each project has helped me get better at using data to answer business questions.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
This shows some examples of the AI-generated content and how I edited it.


I asked ChatGPT: “What pages should I include in a portfolio website for an MBA student?”
The AI suggested: - Home/About page - Resume page
- Projects page - Skills page - Contact page
I kept this structure because it made sense and covered everything I needed to show.



AI’s first draft: “Hello! I’m an MBA student with a passion for data analytics. I enjoy working with data and solving business problems.”
Problem: Way too generic and didn’t sound like me at all.
My revision: “I’m currently pursuing my MBA at University Canada West, focusing on business analytics and data-driven decision making. What excites me most is taking complex business problems and finding insights through data analysis.”
I made it more specific and conversational.



AI’s draft: “Worked on various data analysis projects. Helped with forecasting and financial analysis.”
Problem: Too vague, didn’t explain what I actually did.
My version: “Worked with senior analysts on various data projects, mainly focused on sales forecasting and financial analysis. My role involved analyzing historical data, creating visualizations, and helping present findings to stakeholders.”
Added actual details about the work I did.



AI’s draft: “This was a project about BYD entering the Canadian market. I analyzed the electric vehicle market and looked at competition.”
Problem: Way too short and didn’t show what I actually learned or did.
My version: “For my MBA program, I worked on analyzing how BYD could enter the Canadian electric vehicle market. This was a really interesting project because it involved looking at everything from competitive dynamics to regulatory challenges.”
Made it more detailed and showed what made the project interesting.



AI’s draft: Just listed: “Python, Excel, Tableau, Business Analytics, Problem Solving, Communication”
Problem: Just a list with no context.
My version: Organized into categories (Technical Skills, Analytical Abilities, Business Skills) and added brief descriptions of what I use each tool for.
This gave more context about my actual capabilities.



The AI gave me a good starting structure, but I had to: - Rewrite almost everything to sound like me - Add all the specific details about my actual work and projects - Make the tone more conversational and less formal - Remove generic phrases that could apply to anyone - Add personal touches about what I find interesting
The technical stuff (CSS, Markdown formatting) the AI handled well. The content needed a lot of work to make it authentic.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
“Create a site map for a professional portfolio website for an MBA student interested in business analytics and data analysis.”



Suggested Site Structure: 1. Home/About Page - Personal introduction and professional overview 2. Resume/CV Page - Education, experience, and qualifications 3. Projects Page - Showcase of academic and professional projects 4. Skills Page - Technical and soft skills inventory 5. Contact Page - Contact information and networking opportunities
Additional Recommendations: - Include navigation menu for easy access - Add downloadable resume option - Incorporate professional photo - Include links to LinkedIn and other professional profiles - Ensure mobile responsiveness - Use consistent branding and color scheme



Accepted - This structure was comprehensive and aligned with portfolio best practices. No changes were needed to the overall architecture.

## BYD Market Entry Strategy – Canada

### Overview
This MBA project analyzed how BYD could successfully enter the Canadian electric vehicle market. The project combined market research, competitive analysis, and strategic planning to develop a comprehensive market entry strategy.

### Project Focus:
- Market entry strategy for BYD in the Canadian automotive sector
- Competitive analysis with Tesla and other EV manufacturers
- Analysis of foreign policies impacting international expansion
- Trade tariff evaluation and regulatory considerations
- Launch opportunities such as the Vancouver International Auto Expo
- Assessment of Canadian consumer preferences and market trends

### Methodology:
- Conducted comprehensive market research on Canadian EV landscape
- Analyzed competitive positioning and market share data
- Evaluated regulatory environment and policy implications
- Assessed market entry barriers and opportunities

### Outcome:
The project demonstrated how analytics and market research can help companies expand globally and compete in new markets. Key findings included strategic timing recommendations, optimal market positioning strategies, and actionable insights for overcoming entry barriers.




Added comprehensive overview to establish context
Included specific analytical components (competitive analysis, policy analysis)
Added methodology section to demonstrate analytical approach
Included outcome section to show value and insights
Demonstrated strategic thinking and business acumen

title: Reflection on Using AI Tools for Portfolio Development
text:
When I first started thinking about how to structure a portfolio website, I honestly wasn’t sure where to begin. I used ChatGPT to help brainstorm what pages I should include and what kind of content would make sense for each section. The AI suggested a five-page layout (Home, Resume, Projects, Skills, Contact) which seemed pretty standard for portfolios, so I went with that.
The AI was helpful in giving me a starting framework. It suggested what should go on each page - like putting highlights on the home page and organizing my resume in a traditional format. This saved me time because I didn’t have to research portfolio best practices from scratch.

title: Reflection on Using AI Tools for Portfolio Development
text:
Once I had the structure, I asked the AI to help draft content for each page. This is where things got interesting. The AI created initial drafts that were… okay, but very generic. For example, when describing my work at Appsyco, it used really formal language and made everything sound more impressive than it actually was.
I had to go through and rewrite a lot of it to sound like me and to be accurate about what I actually did. I added specific details about the forecasting projects I worked on and the dividend analysis tasks. The AI couldn’t know these details - only I could provide them.
The tone was another issue. The AI writes in this very polished, professional way that doesn’t really sound like how I would naturally explain my experience. I spent time making it more conversational while still keeping it professional enough for a portfolio.

text:
The biggest advantage was speed. The AI generated a complete website structure and initial content in maybe 15-20 minutes. If I had started from scratch, it probably would have taken me several hours just to figure out the layout and write first drafts.
The AI was also really good at technical stuff like creating the CSS styling and making sure all the Markdown formatting was correct. I don’t have much web development experience, so having the AI handle the styling saved me from having to learn CSS in depth.

The main limitation was that AI can’t actually know anything about my personal experiences. Every specific detail about my education, my projects, and my work had to come from me. The AI could create a template, but I had to fill in the real substance.
Another challenge was that the AI couldn’t help me decide what to emphasize or how to position myself for the kind of jobs I want. Those strategic decisions required me to think about my career goals and what employers in analytics are looking for.

title: Reflection on Using AI Tools for Portfolio Development
text:
This project showed me that AI tools are really useful for getting started and handling technical tasks, but they can’t replace the human element. The final portfolio needed my personal touch, my actual experiences, and my judgment about what matters.
I think the best approach is using AI as a starting point and then putting in the work to make it authentic and personal. The AI helped me build something faster than I could have alone, but the value comes from the parts that are genuinely mine.
(Word count: 498)

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
I asked ChatGPT: “What pages should I include in a portfolio website for an MBA student?”
The AI suggested: - Home/About page - Resume page
- Projects page - Skills page - Contact page
I kept this structure because it made sense and covered everything I needed to show.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
AI’s first draft: “Hello! I’m an MBA student with a passion for data analytics. I enjoy working with data and solving business problems.”
Problem: Way too generic and didn’t sound like me at all.
My revision: “I’m currently pursuing my MBA at University Canada West, focusing on business analytics and data-driven decision making. What excites me most is taking complex business problems and finding insights through data analysis.”
I made it more specific and conversational.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
AI’s draft: “Worked on various data analysis projects. Helped with forecasting and financial analysis.”
Problem: Too vague, didn’t explain what I actually did.
My version: “Worked with senior analysts on various data projects, mainly focused on sales forecasting and financial analysis. My role involved analyzing historical data, creating visualizations, and helping present findings to stakeholders.”
Added actual details about the work I did.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
AI’s draft: “This was a project about BYD entering the Canadian market. I analyzed the electric vehicle market and looked at competition.”
Problem: Way too short and didn’t show what I actually learned or did.
My version: “For my MBA program, I worked on analyzing how BYD could enter the Canadian electric vehicle market. This was a really interesting project because it involved looking at everything from competitive dynamics to regulatory challenges.”
Made it more detailed and showed what made the project interesting.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
AI’s draft: Just listed: “Python, Excel, Tableau, Business Analytics, Problem Solving, Communication”
Problem: Just a list with no context.
My version: Organized into categories (Technical Skills, Analytical Abilities, Business Skills) and added brief descriptions of what I use each tool for.
This gave more context about my actual capabilities.

title: Appendix: AI Drafts and Revisions
text:
The AI gave me a good starting structure, but I had to: - Rewrite almost everything to sound like me - Add all the specific details about my actual work and projects - Make the tone more conversational and less formal - Remove generic phrases that could apply to anyone - Add personal touches about what I find interesting
The technical stuff (CSS, Markdown formatting) the AI handled well. The content needed a lot of work to make it authentic.
